name: Zinneera and Aflah
title: Zinneera and Aflah (RA): The Tortured Ones
meta_description: The story of Zinneera and Aflah (may Allah be pleased with them), two enslaved early Muslims of Makkah tortured for their faith. A blind woman whose sight was returned, and a man freed by Abu Bakr.
keywords: Zinneera, Aflah, story of Zinneera, enslaved Muslims of Makkah, Abu Bakr freeing slaves, persecution in Makkah, sahaba stories
lede: Some of the earliest believers left us no last name, sometimes no first name at all. They had no clan to protect them and no power in the eyes of their city. What they had was certainty, and they paid for it with their bodies.

This is the story of two of them: Zinneera and Aflah (may Allah be pleased with them), enslaved in Makkah, tortured for saying that there is only one God, and remembered for a faith that did not bend.

sections.h2: The ones with no one to protect them
paras: When we look back at the first Muslims, the names we know best often belong to people of standing. But beneath them was another group: the enslaved, the poor, the ones with no tribe to shield them. Of many of these believers we have only a single line, or a first name, or nothing at all.

These were the people who put everything on the line in Makkah without seeing any light at the end of the tunnel. No protection. No promise that things would get easier in their lifetime. Only the conviction that what they had found was true. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ saw their suffering and longed to do more for them than he was able to do in those early years.

Some of them lived long, honoured lives and went on to Madinah, even past the death of the Prophet ﷺ. Others were killed under the persecution itself, dying as they were tortured, and a number of them died without leaving us their names.
Allah knows each of them. They were forerunners, among the very first of this ummah to give themselves to it. Two of these believers come down to us with stories worth holding onto.

sections.h2: Abu Bakr, who bought their freedom
paras: Almost all of these stories pass through one man: Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him). When he saw a person being tortured for the sake of Allah, especially the enslaved, he would go and pay whatever it took to purchase their freedom.

The disbelievers questioned his motives. Even his own father told him that these were people who could be of no use to him, that his money was wasted on them. Abu Bakr answered that he was not seeking anything from them at all. He was seeking something from Allah, a reward that only Allah could give and that no person of this world could ever repay.
It is said that he freed several people before he made his journey to Madinah. Some of their names are known to us. Among those he rescued from that cruelty was the man we will come to know as Aflah.

sections.h2: A handful of names we still keep
paras: Before we come to the two, it is worth pausing on the others, because they fill in the world these believers lived in. Among the enslaved women freed for the sake of Allah was one who had been brutally mistreated by Umar ibn al-Khattab in the days before his Islam, beaten until her tormentor grew tired. Her freedom too was purchased.

There was a woman named Lubaynah who took the wrath of the powerful, all for the sake of Allah. There were others whose names survive only in passing, and many more whose names did not survive at all.

It is a sobering thing to sit with. A whole layer of the first believers gave everything, and history barely recorded that they existed. We do not know what they looked like or where they were buried, or what words passed their lips in their final moments. We only know that they held on, and that Allah, who needs no record, knows every one of them by name.

sections.h2: Zinneera, beaten until she could not see
paras: Zinneera was an enslaved woman in Makkah, and to be enslaved there did not only put you at the mercy of any cruel person. It often put you at the mercy of the worst of them. She fell under the power of Abu Jahl, of the clan of Makhzum.

He singled her out. There are narrations from more than one person about the particular harshness he showed her, harassing her and beating her publicly and in ugly ways. This was the same hatred that took the life of the first martyr of this ummah, and Zinneera too was marked for that kind of cruelty.
He beat her until she lost her eyesight. He beat her until she became blind. Such was the severity of the blows that fell on this woman who had done nothing but believe.

Then he twisted even that. When people spoke about what had happened to her, he claimed it was not him. It was the idols she had rejected, he said, that had taken her sight away as punishment for turning from them.

Picture her position for a moment. She had been beaten to blindness in the name of these idols. She held no rank in society and had no one to defend her. And still, blind and broken, she answered him.

She said they were lying. By Allah, she said, no harm comes from these idols. They do not benefit anyone. They have no power at all. It is Allah who holds all power, from whom every benefit comes, and in whose will alone any harm can take place.

Think of the strength in that. Stripped of her sight, with nothing left to lose and no audience to impress, she still refused to grant these stones a single ounce of the power her tormentor claimed for them.

When she said it, Allah returned her sight to her. She could see again. The ones who had mocked her now had no honest explanation. The same idols they had credited with her blindness could hardly be credited with her healing. So they reached for the only thing left and called it sorcery, a proof of the magic of the Prophet ﷺ. But it was no such thing. It was a quiet gift from Allah to a woman whose certainty had not wavered for an instant, an answer to her words from the only One who could give one.

The other believer is best described by who he resembled. Aflah was tortured in the same way as Bilal, by the same man, Umayyah ibn Khalaf. If you want to picture his ordeal, picture everything that was done to Bilal, and know that it was done to him too.

He was tortured in the scorching heat. He was forced to lie out in the desert with a heavy stone pressed onto his chest until it nearly killed him. He was deprived of food and water. He was deprived of sleep. He was lashed in public, again and again, as he held his ground and refused to leave his faith.
The last we hear of his torment is the worst of it. The pagans tied a rope around his feet and his neck and dragged his nearly lifeless body through the streets of Makkah until he was almost dead.

Abu Bakr saw him in that state and bought his freedom. Then, unlike Bilal who stayed with Abu Bakr in Makkah and went on to Madinah, Aflah was sent to Abyssinia to escape the heavy persecution. According to the few accounts we have, he died there soon after.
We carry very little information about Zinneera and Aflah. A few lines, a handful of narrations. Yet what they endured is part of the reason this beautiful religion reached us at all.

There is a small story Dr. Omar Suleiman tells about Aflah. He once met a man who had named his child after this tortured companion, and the man did not know who the companion was. When he was told that this was a person tortured the way Bilal was tortured, it brought him to tears.

That is the point. These are names worth knowing and worth using. We should say them and think about the people behind them: a woman who answered her torturer while blind, and a man who endured the desert and the rope rather than deny his Lord. To name a child after one of them, or simply to remember them, is to keep a thread unbroken back to the very first days of this faith.
Behind each of them is a level of certainty that held firm under the worst that Makkah could do, and a place with Allah that we can only try to imagine.

lessons_intro: Their stories are short, only a few lines each, but they carry something most of us are quietly looking for: a faith that holds when there is no reward in sight and no one watching.

lessons.bold: Certainty does not need an audience.
lessons.text: Zinneera had no rank, no protector, and no platform. Blind and beaten, she still answered her torturer plainly: the idols have no power, only Allah does. Real conviction is what you hold when no one is impressed by it and nothing is gained from it.

lessons.bold: Worth is not measured by usefulness.
lessons.text: Abu Bakr was told plainly that these people could do nothing for him. He freed them anyway, seeking only Allah. The people the world steps over are often the ones nearest to God, and treating them as disposable is the error of those who tortured them.

lessons.bold: Some are not spared the hardship.
lessons.text: Aflah was rescued from the rope, then sent away, and died far from home soon after. Not every faithful life ends in visible relief or a happy turn. Their reward was never meant to arrive in this world, and that does not make it any smaller.

lessons.bold: We inherited this from people we barely know.
lessons.text: Much of what we practice today was carried through the bodies of believers whose names we have lost. We pray and fast in the light they bought with their suffering. Gratitude, and saying their names when we have them, is the least we owe them.

faq.a: They were among the enslaved early Muslims of Makkah who were tortured for accepting Islam. Zinneera (may Allah be pleased with her) was a woman beaten until she lost her sight, and Aflah (may Allah be pleased with him) was a man tortured in the same way as Bilal.

Abu Jahl beat her until she became blind, then claimed her own rejected idols had punished her. She answered that the idols had no power, that only Allah does. When she said this, Allah returned her sight to her.

faq.q: How was Aflah connected to Bilal?
faq.a: Aflah was tortured by the same man who tortured Bilal, Umayyah ibn Khalaf, and in the same brutal ways. Abu Bakr bought his freedom and sent him to Abyssinia, where he is reported to have died soon after.

faq.q: What can we learn from their lives?
faq.a: That sincere certainty needs no audience, that the people the world overlooks can be the closest to Allah, and that we received this faith through believers who suffered for it before us.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is easy to imagine that real faith is for people with a voice, a following, some way to be seen. Zinneera and Aflah had none of that. They were enslaved and overlooked, and what they held onto was held in private, in the heat of the desert and under the blows of a tormentor, where only Allah was watching. That is exactly what makes it stay with us.

Most of our own tests are quiet ones too: a truth to hold when no one would notice if we let it go, a kindness no one will thank us for, a moment of patience that no one sees. Their lives ask a simple thing of us today. Be certain when it costs you something. Value the people the world ignores rather than measuring them by what they can do for you. And remember, in your prayer, the ones who carried this faith to you with their bodies and their names half forgotten, and ask Allah to be pleased with them as He was pleased with their patience.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of these companions in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported. Some details about these companions are preserved only in brief.

slides:
Some of the first believers left us no last name.

No clan to protect them. No power in their city.

What they had was certainty, and they paid for it with their bodies.

When Abu Bakr saw someone tortured for the sake of Allah, he bought their freedom.

His own father said these people were of no use to him.

"I am not seeking anything from them. I am seeking Allah."

Zinneera was enslaved in Makkah and fell into the hands of Abu Jahl.

He singled her out. He beat her publicly.

He beat her until she became blind.

Then he twisted it.

It was not me, he said. It was the idols she rejected that took her sight.

Blind, beaten, with no one to defend her, she answered him.

"They are lying. By Allah, these idols have no power. They benefit no one. It is Allah who holds all power."

When she said it, Allah returned her sight.

She could see again.

Her enemies had no honest answer, so they called it magic. It was a gift from Allah to a woman whose certainty never wavered.

Aflah was tortured like Bilal, by the same man, in the same ways.

Scorching heat. A stone on his chest. Dragged through Makkah by a rope.

Abu Bakr freed him and sent him to Abyssinia, where he died soon after.

**Certainty does not need an audience.**

They had no rank and no platform. They held the truth anyway, where only Allah was watching.

May Allah be pleased with them.
social.caption:
Some of the earliest Muslims left us no last name, and a few left no name at all. They were enslaved, poor, and unprotected, and they were tortured for saying there is only one God.

Zinneera (may Allah be pleased with her) was beaten by Abu Jahl until she lost her sight. Still she told him plainly that his idols had no power, that only Allah does. And Allah returned her sight to her. Aflah (may Allah be pleased with him) was tortured exactly as Bilal was, until Abu Bakr bought his freedom.

We inherited this faith through people like them. Which part stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: Beaten until she was blind, she still spoke the truth
email.alt_subject: The believers we inherited this faith from
email.preview: Two enslaved Muslims of Makkah, tortured for their faith, and a sight that was returned.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Some of the first Muslims left us no last name. A few left no name at all. They were enslaved in Makkah, poor, and unprotected, and they were tortured for saying there is only one God.

When Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him) saw a person being tortured for the sake of Allah, he would buy their freedom. His own father told him these were people who could do nothing for him. Abu Bakr answered that he was not seeking anything from them. He was seeking something from Allah that no one in this world could repay.

Two of these believers stay with you once you hear them.

The first is Zinneera (may Allah be pleased with her). She fell into the hands of Abu Jahl, who singled her out and beat her publicly, until she lost her eyesight. Then he claimed it was not him. It was the idols she had rejected, he said, that had blinded her.

Blind and beaten, with no one to defend her, she answered him. She said they were lying. By Allah, these idols have no power. They benefit no one. It is Allah who holds all power. And when she said it, Allah returned her sight to her. Her enemies had no honest explanation, so they called it magic. It was a quiet gift from Allah to a woman whose certainty had not wavered.

The second is Aflah (may Allah be pleased with him). He was tortured the way Bilal was tortured, by the same man, in the same brutal ways: scorching heat, a heavy stone on his chest, dragged through the streets by a rope until he was nearly lifeless. Abu Bakr bought his freedom and sent him to Abyssinia, where he died soon after.

There is a lesson here that is easy to miss. Certainty does not need an audience. They held the truth where no one was impressed by it, where only Allah was watching. Much of what we practice today reached us through believers exactly like them.

May Allah be pleased with them, and gather us in the company of the ones who suffered so that we could inherit this faith.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Some of the earliest believers left us no last name, and a few left no name at all. They had no clan to protect them and no power in the eyes of their city. What they had was certainty, and they paid for it with their bodies.

When Abu Bakr saw a person being tortured for the sake of Allah, he would pay whatever it took to free them. His own father told him these were people who could do nothing for him. Abu Bakr said he was not seeking anything from them at all. He was seeking Allah.

One of those he could not protect at first was a woman named Zinneera. Enslaved in Makkah, she fell into the hands of Abu Jahl, who singled her out and beat her publicly, until she lost her sight. Then he twisted even that, claiming it was the idols she had rejected that had blinded her.

Blind and beaten, with no one to defend her, she answered him. She said he was lying. By Allah, she said, these idols have no power. They benefit no one. It is Allah who holds all power, and in His will alone can any harm take place.

When she said it, Allah returned her sight to her. She could see again. Those who had mocked her had no honest answer, so they called it sorcery. It was no such thing. It was a quiet gift from Allah to a woman whose certainty had not bent.

The other is Aflah, who was tortured the way Bilal was tortured, by the same man, in the same ways. He was laid in the scorching desert with a heavy stone on his chest, starved, lashed, and at the last dragged through Makkah by a rope around his feet and neck. Abu Bakr bought his freedom and sent him to Abyssinia, where he died soon after.
We carry only a few lines about either of them. Yet this faith reached us in part through people exactly like them, whose names we barely know and whose suffering we can barely imagine.
storybook.pullquote: They are lying. By Allah, these idols have no power.
storybook.reflection: It is easy to think real faith belongs to people with a voice and a following. Zinneera and Aflah had neither. They were enslaved and overlooked, and they held the truth in private, where only Allah was watching. Their lives ask us to be certain when it costs us something, and to honour the people the world ignores.
storybook.source: Their story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
